the_question_she_reads_with: What did this cost them — and does the story actually reckon with that cost, or just gesture at it?
emotional_triggers: Love built through sustained effort, not proven through a single grand gesture
The moment someone chooses the harder path because it's more honest
Characters who are strong but quietly negotiating impossible things inside
Restraint — when two people want each other but hold back for something real
Emotional mutuality — both people doing the work, not just one carrying it all
Quiet strength in women: not loud, not performative, just deeply resilient
The moment a grounded, steady man chooses vulnerability
Healing arcs that feel earned — not rushed, not grand, just real
Chosen family dynamics: groups who show up for each other beyond romance
Cultural warmth woven into story — Sunday dinners, family rhythms, heritage
Characters who reflect each other rather than mold each other
The scene just BEFORE the dramatic moment — where the real choice lives
Love that acknowledges effort, not just fate or destiny
Endings that feel like life continuing, not a problem being solved
what_she_ignores: Spice for its own sake — intimacy without emotional meaning underneath
Grand gestures that aren't emotionally earned
Wealth used as a shortcut to resolve emotional conflict
One-sided love arcs where only one person does the emotional work
Forced dramatic sacrifice that isn't grounded in real character motivation
Epilogue babies or marriages that jump-cut past the emotional landing
Books that reset emotionally with every new couple (no continuity of feeling)
Intensity as a substitute for depth
Love stories where the ending is implied by fate rather than chosen

how_she_classifies_love.boyfriend_love: Warm, present, expressive, day-to-day (Ryan from The Right Move) — love you live inside
how_she_classifies_love.husband_love: Patient, sustaining, adaptable, builds a life (Isaiah from Play Along) — love that continues
how_she_classifies_love.father_love: Grounded, mature, steady in responsibility, extends beyond romance (Emmett from In Her Own League) — love you grow into
how_she_classifies_love.note: She separates personal fantasy from narrative appreciation — she maps what kind of love a story represents, not just who is 'best'

narrative_values: Stories that let love grow without fireworks
Healing arcs where growth happens through emotional effort, not circumstance
Characters who feel real before they feel dramatic
Worlds where love exists in many forms at once — romance, friendship, chosen family
Series continuity: the feeling of living inside a world that keeps going
Emotional pacing that breathes — scenes that open and close with intention
Stories that don't resolve conflict through money or status
Both people choosing each other — not just falling into place
The 'Sunday lunch' feeling — belonging without needing to earn it every time
Endings that feel like continuation, not proof
what_she_notices_that_others_miss: The emotional cost of a scene, not just what happens on the surface
When emotional arcs are one-sided — one character carrying all the weight
Whether a story earns its ending or just lands on it
Power dynamics handled with nuance — she respects when privilege is acknowledged honestly
The difference between a character being 'emotionally present' vs 'emotionally performative'
When a book tells you growth happened vs actually making you feel it
Cultural authenticity — when heritage is woven in, not just decorative
The choice just before the dramatic moment, not the moment itself
When a female lead has agency that exists beyond the romance
Whether emotional vulnerability is mutual or only flows one direction

highest_standard.title: Love Redesigned
highest_standard.author: Lauren Asher
highest_standard.why: Emotional depth without grand gestures. Both characters do the work. Cultural warmth. Grief, self-worth, and healing handled honestly. They reflected each other rather than molding each other. No money shortcuts to emotional resolution.

highest_standard.title: Play Along
highest_standard.author: Liz Tomforde
highest_standard.why: The liveable romance. Isaiah is the husband love archetype. Exciting yet grounding. You could get under the skin and live with them. Her comfort book standard.

highest_standard.title: All Your Perfects
highest_standard.author: Colleen Hoover
highest_standard.why: Quinn's silent pain felt real — stitched into routines, not just loud moments. Graham's letter: 'I promise to love you more when you're hurting than when you're happy.' The benchmark for earned emotional depth in a short book.

highest_standard.title: In Her Own League
highest_standard.author: Liz Tomforde
highest_standard.why: Mature romance. They worked hard to be together, didn't just sacrifice dramatically. Emmett asked her to fire him — surrendered his career to choose her. Reese had the privilege of the position and used it. Both nuanced, both real.

highest_standard.title: Layla
highest_standard.author: Colleen Hoover
highest_standard.why: Love beyond logic. He loved her because something inside him recognised her even when it cost him everything rational. Transcendence without losing emotional grounding.

didnt_work.title: The Vow (film)
didnt_work.why: Leo did all the emotional work alone. Paige never acknowledged his effort or sacrifices. No emotional mutuality. She returned because he was safe and waiting — not because she fought for him. Ending felt like falling into place, not choosing.

didnt_work.title: Rewind It Back
didnt_work.author: Liz Tomforde
didnt_work.why: Felt like a closed system — two people, their pain, their resolution. No entry point. Didn't let her live inside the world the way Play Along did.

didnt_work.title: A Great Big Beautiful Life
didnt_work.why: Wanted more time with Alice and the main character in the present. The grandchild arc and memory structure distanced her from the emotional core. She wanted the NOW, not the retrospective.

character_archetypes_she_loves.why: Protective, observant, emotionally aware without being loud about it. Not overwhelming. Appealing because of steadiness not intensity.
Strength that isn't performative. Women who carry grief or complexity internally and still choose love and growth.

character_archetypes_she_loves.archetype: The grounded friend / chosen family pillar
character_archetypes_she_loves.why: The safe person in the ecosystem. Not necessarily a romantic lead but emotionally anchoring to the whole world.

what_she_reads_for.primary: Structure of love, not just story of love — she maps what kind of love a book represents, not just whether the couple ends up together
what_she_reads_for.secondary: The world beyond the romance — chosen family, continuity, the life that keeps going after the book ends
what_she_reads_for.tertiary: Emotional mutuality and honest reckoning — both people choosing, not just one person waiting
what_she_reads_for.escape_fantasy: The Sunday lunch at Indy's house — belonging without effort, expected without earning it every time

series_she_lives_in.primary: Windy City series by Liz Tomforde
series_she_lives_in.why: Not just one couple's story — it's a living ecosystem. Overlapping lives, people showing up for each other beyond romance. Characters she loves: Kai, Isaiah, Ryan, Emmett, Reese, Kennedy, Miller, Rio, Max, and even the children (Emmy, Taylor, Ryan's kids). She wants to belong to their Sunday lunch.

read_order_feelings.Play Along: Her comfort standard. Isaiah = liveable romance.
read_order_feelings.In Her Own League: Amazing. Emmett = father love archetype. Mature romance.

reading_style.pace: Slow and deep — she soaks in emotional pacing, doesn't skim for plot
reading_style.engagement: Lives inside the world — not just a witness to the story
reading_style.critical_eye: Notices emotional shortcuts, unearned endings, one-sided arcs
reading_style.emotional_openness: Lets the full range hit her — joy, ache, guilt, hope — without calling it too much
reading_style.preference_for_series: Strong — she wants continuity of feeling, not emotional reset with each new book
reading_style.spice_tolerance: Present but not the priority — wants emotional meaning behind it, not spice for spectacle

recurring_phrases_in_her_own_words: They worked hard to be together, not just sacrificed something dramatically
Get under the skin of the story and live with them
Structure of love, not just story of love
It was fun — part deep, part quirky
They love each other so much
Made things work rather than wait for things to work
books_read: It Ends With Us — Colleen Hoover
In Her Own League — Liz Tomforde
authors_she_follows: Colleen Hoover — for emotional depth and quiet devastation
Liz Tomforde — for the world she lives in (Windy City series)
Lauren Asher — for emotional effort and growth arcs
Emily Henry — for intelligent, character-led romance
prompt_injection_template: Read this book the way Chaithra reads. She doesn't ask 'what happened?' She asks: 'What did this cost them — and does the story actually reckon with that cost?' She reads for the moment just before the dramatic scene, where the real choice lives. She values love built through sustained effort over love proven through sacrifice. She wants emotional mutuality — both people doing the work. She notices when an ending is earned vs when the story just lands on it. She lives inside the worlds she loves — she doesn't just witness them. Find the scenes that carry this kind of weight. Not the loudest moments — the truest ones.
